title: State building: Rise of Chinese dynasties
description: Answer passage-based questions about Emperor Qin Shi Huang.
topic_path: State building: Rise of Chinese dynasties

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question that follows. 

> "[Historians] hold it a mark of fame to defy the ruler, regard it as lofty to take a dissenting stance, and they lead the lesser officials in fabricating slander. If behaviour such as this is not prohibited, then in upper circles the authority of the ruler will be compromised, and in lower ones cliques will form. Therefore it should be prohibited.
	
> 'I therefore request that all records of the historians other than those of the state of Qin be burned. With the exception of the academicians whose duty it is to possess them, if there are persons anywhere in the empire who have in their possession copies of the Odes, the Documents, or the writings of the 
hundred schools of philosophy, they shall in all cases deliver them to the governor or his commandant [overseer] for burning. Anyone who ventures to discuss the Odes or Documents shall be executed in the marketplace. Anyone who uses antiquity to criticize the present shall be executed along with his family.'"

Sima Qian quoting EmperorQin Shi Huang in the _Annals of the Great Historian_


**Why might Qin Shi Huang have wanted to suppress historians?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Emperor probably didn't think historians had the political ability to band together and overthrow him on their own.

choices.content: He may have felt that by comparing him to previous Chinese rulers, scholars could have sought to delegitimize his reign
He thought they would band together and attempt to install a new emperor
He thought that the Zhou-era texts inherently discredited him
He was upset with their unwillingness to help implement his common written script

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question that follows. 

> "[Historians] hold it a mark of fame to defy the ruler, regard it as lofty to take a dissenting stance, and they lead the lesser officials in fabricating slander. If behaviour such as this is not prohibited, then in upper circles the authority of the ruler will be compromised, and in lower ones cliques will form. Therefore it should be prohibited.
	
> 'I therefore request that all records of the historians other than those of the state of Qin be burned. With the exception of the academicians whose duty it is to possess them, if there are persons anywhere in the empire who have in their possession copies of the Odes, the Documents, or the writings of the 
hundred schools of philosophy, they shall in all cases deliver them to the governor or his commandant [overseer] for burning. Anyone who ventures to discuss the Odes or Documents shall be executed in the marketplace. Anyone who uses antiquity to criticize the present shall be executed along with his family.'"

Sima Qian quoting EmperorQin Shi Huang in the _Annals of the Great Historian_

**What would burning philosophy and history texts help Qin Shi Huang achieve?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Although the Emperor wanted to achieve immortality, he didn't think that burning books would help him achieve this.

choices.content: A sense of Daoist peace through ignorance
A chance to keep his administrators busy with hunting down banned books
A control over information that the citizens of his empire had access to
He hoped it would help him achieve immortality
